title: River Blythe

infobox.value: [Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands")—[Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent")—[Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber")—[North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea")

flows through the [English Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(England) "Midlands (England)") from central [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), through the [Borough of Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull") and on to [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire") in north Warwickshire. It runs along the [Meriden Gap](/wiki/Meriden_Gap "Meriden Gap") in the [Midlands Plateau](/wiki/Midlands_Plateau "Midlands Plateau"), is fed by the [River Cole](/wiki/River_Cole,_West_Midlands "River Cole, West Midlands") and is a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands") beside the [West Midland Bird Club](/wiki/West_Midland_Bird_Club "West Midland Bird Club")'s [Ladywalk reserve](/wiki/Ladywalk_reserve "Ladywalk reserve"). This then joins the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), whose waters reach the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") via the [Humber Estuary](/wiki/Humber "Humber").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the river in the Midlands. For similarly named rivers, see [River Blyth (disambiguation)](/wiki/River_Blyth_(disambiguation) "River Blyth (disambiguation)").

River in Warwickshire and the West Midlands, England

The **River Blythe** flows through the [English Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(England) "Midlands (England)") from central [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), through the [Borough of Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull") and on to [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire") in north Warwickshire. It runs along the [Meriden Gap](/wiki/Meriden_Gap "Meriden Gap") in the [Midlands Plateau](/wiki/Midlands_Plateau "Midlands Plateau"), is fed by the [River Cole](/wiki/River_Cole,_West_Midlands "River Cole, West Midlands") and is a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands") beside the [West Midland Bird Club](/wiki/West_Midland_Bird_Club "West Midland Bird Club")'s [Ladywalk reserve](/wiki/Ladywalk_reserve "Ladywalk reserve"). This then joins the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), whose waters reach the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") via the [Humber Estuary](/wiki/Humber "Humber").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Brueton_Park_-_geograph.org.uk_-_559259.jpg/250px-Brueton_Park_-_geograph.org.uk_-_559259.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brueton_Park_-_geograph.org.uk_-_559259.jpg)

[Brueton Park](/wiki/Malvern_%26_Brueton_Park "Malvern & Brueton Park") Lake, formed by the damming of the River Blythe at [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull")

The [river](/wiki/River "River") rises at various sources near [Earlswood Lakes](/wiki/Earlswood_Lakes "Earlswood Lakes"), but the principal tributary is listed as Spring Brook[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) ([52°21′03″N 1°50′37″W﻿ / ﻿52.350915°N 1.843601°W﻿ / 52.350915; -1.843601](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=River_Blythe&params=52.350915_N_1.843601_W_region:GB_type:landmark); [grid reference](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid "Ordnance Survey National Grid") [SP107725](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=River_Blythe&params=52.350467_N_1.844336_W_region:GB_scale:25000)). From here it winds north east, skirting [Cheswick Green](/wiki/Cheswick_Green "Cheswick Green"), towards [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull"). Here it passes through a local wildlife park, [Malvern and Brueton Park and Nature Reserve](/wiki/Malvern_%26_Brueton_Park "Malvern & Brueton Park"), where it briefly splits/outpours for approximately 250 metres to form Brueton Park Lake. From here it meanders east, past several old country houses—Old Berry Hall, Ravenshaw Hall and Eastcote Hall—before turning sharply south towards [Barston](/wiki/Barston "Barston"), which is encircled within a large meander of the river. At [Temple Balsall](/wiki/Temple_Balsall "Temple Balsall") the Cuttle Brook feeds the river, which now arcs north, and again close by Barston. Past [Hampton in Arden](/wiki/Hampton_in_Arden "Hampton in Arden") the river is fed by *Shadow Brook*, at *Diddington Hall*. Travelling further north it passes to the east of [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire"), and is fed by the [River Cole](/wiki/River_Cole,_West_Midlands "River Cole, West Midlands"), only a few hundred yards before it itself feeds into the [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands") ([52°31′20″N 1°41′18″W﻿ / ﻿52.522317°N 1.688242°W﻿ / 52.522317; -1.688242](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=River_Blythe&params=52.522317_N_1.688242_W_region:GB_type:landmark); [grid reference](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid "Ordnance Survey National Grid") [SP212916](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=River_Blythe&params=52.521870_N_1.688982_W_region:GB_scale:25000)), at Ladywalk Nature Reserve.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Blythe_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_65387.jpg/250px-Blythe_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_65387.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blythe_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_65387.jpg)

Blythe Valley, looking north along the River Blythe near Eastcote

The river is "a particularly fine example of a lowland river on clay" according to its [Sites of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest "Sites of Special Scientific Interest") citation. The SSSI was notified in 1989, and includes a 24-mile (39 km) stretch of the river from near its source to the point it joins the River Tame.[[2]](#cite_note-en-2) The Blythe has a wide range of natural geographical features such as riffles, pools, small cliffs and meanders, combined with a high diversity of substrate types ranging from fine silt and clay in the lower reaches to sands and gravels in the upper and middle reaches. The structure of this river is very variable and diverse, and is important as a rare example of such in lowland Britain.[[2]](#cite_note-en-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/The_old_Packhorse_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_50348.jpg/250px-The_old_Packhorse_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_50348.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_old_Packhorse_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_50348.jpg)

The old Packhorse Bridge, south of [Hampton-in-Arden](/wiki/Hampton-in-Arden "Hampton-in-Arden"), crossing the River Blythe

The diverse physical features of the Blythe are matched by its diverse plant communities. Botanically, the Blythe is one of the richest rivers in lowland England, with the most species-rich sections containing as many species as the very richest chalk streams.[[2]](#cite_note-en-2) In addition, several damp, unimproved meadows occur along the length of the river; they receive some of their water from annual flooding and are largely dependent upon the river for the maintenance of a high water-table.[[2]](#cite_note-en-2) As well as the rich and diverse flora, the river supports a diverse invertebrate community with a wide range of [molluscs](/wiki/Molluscs "Molluscs"), [oligochaetes](/wiki/Oligochaetes "Oligochaetes") and [caddisflies](/wiki/Caddisfly "Caddisfly").[[2]](#cite_note-en-2)

However, physical modification to the watercourse and the riparian zone has impacted the functionality of the River Blythe, resulting in the SSSI's condition labelled as "unfavourable no change" in 2017. [[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The factors leading to this were mainly due to historic management; many sections of the river have been straightened, and the riparian zone modified and disrupted, upsetting the site's ecological health and lowering the overall SSSI status.

The river is popular with anglers along much of its length, although in dry weather it can become shallow in places. Species to be found include [perch](/wiki/Perch "Perch"), [chub](/wiki/European_chub "European chub") and [dace](/wiki/Common_Dace "Common Dace") with occasional summer [carp](/wiki/Carp "Carp") being caught. The river also holds [tench](/wiki/Tench "Tench"), [bream](/wiki/Bream "Bream") and [rudd](/wiki/Rudd "Rudd") and more recently a small number of [barbel](/wiki/Barbus_barbus "Barbus barbus") have been caught.
